summary: I was in school on Pearl Harbor Day, December 7th, 1941. At that time, I was only 17 years old and thought the war wouldn't last long. It was a significant event that eventually led to me receiving my draft notice when the draft age was lowered to 18.

I was born in Bridgeville, Delaware, on September 7th, 1924. It’s where I had my first beer at a fireman's parade many years ago, probably in the early '60s. My family roots run deep there, as I come from the Adams family, known as the scrapple boys down in that area.

In 1925, my family moved from Bridgeville to Canterbury, Delaware, where I lived for about three years with my grandmother's cousin. Later, my mother bought a little farm on the Canterbury Milford Road, and I was raised there on that farm. It was a small town but home to me during my childhood.

I was drafted into the United States Army on October 8th, 1943, after turning 18 and losing my deferment from farming. I trained at Camp Croft in South Carolina and was eventually sent overseas as a replacement soldier. Serving in the Army during World War II shaped much of my early adult life.

After being drafted, I first reported to Camp Dix in New Jersey where I stayed only a few days before being sent to basic training. Camp Dix was my initial entry point into the Army before heading south to Camp Croft. It was the start of my military journey in 1943.

I completed 17 weeks of infantry basic training at Camp Croft in Spartanburg, South Carolina, finishing in February 1944. It was tough but prepared me for combat. After training, I went home for a short 10-day delay before moving on to Fort Meade.

Spartanburg, South Carolina, is where Camp Croft is located, and that's where I did my infantry basic training. I spent several months there in late 1943 and early 1944, learning the skills I would need for combat overseas. It was a significant place in my transition from civilian to soldier.

After basic training and a short home leave, I reported to Fort Meade near Baltimore, Maryland. It was there I learned I was part of the next group of replacements going overseas. Fort Meade was a brief but important stop before heading to the war front.

Fort Meade, where I was stationed briefly before going overseas, is near Baltimore, Maryland. While I didn't spend much time in Baltimore itself, it was the closest major city to Fort Meade where I prepared for deployment. This area marked the last stateside stop before heading out.

From Fort Meade, we moved under cover of darkness to Newport News, Virginia, where we boarded a ship to go overseas. I remember arriving there early in the morning and being loaded onto boats after German prisoners were unloaded. Newport News was the last port of call in the U.S. before heading to North Africa.

My first landing overseas was in Casablanca, North Africa. From there, I took a train across to Algiers before boarding another boat to Naples, Italy. Casablanca was my initial introduction to the war theater abroad in early 1944.

After landing in Casablanca, I was transported by train to Algiers, where I boarded a boat to Italy. Algiers served as a key transit point in my journey from North Africa to the Italian front. This was all part of the rapid movement of troops in early 1944.

I landed in Naples, Italy, after traveling through North Africa. From Naples, I took another boat up to Anzio, where I was assigned to combat duty. Naples was a major staging area for the Italian campaign in 1944.

Anzio was where I first saw combat in early April 1944. It was one of the bloodiest battle areas in World War II, lasting from January to May 1944. I was assigned to the 45th Infantry Division there and went straight to the front lines within days of arriving.

My service in the United States Army took place during World War II, starting in 1943. I fought in Italy, including the brutal Anzio campaign and the invasion of southern France. The war shaped my young adult years and involved many intense and dangerous experiences.

After the Anzio campaign, we advanced and took Rome on June 4th, 1944. The pope declared Rome an open city, so fighting ceased there, and the Germans withdrew. It was a major victory and a memorable moment during my service in Italy.

At Anzio, replacements like me were assigned to different divisions including the 3rd Infantry Division. Although I was not assigned to the 3rd, I remember they were one of the main units involved in the fighting there. It was a tough place for all the divisions engaged.

Like the 3rd and 45th, the 36th Infantry Division also received replacements at Anzio. I was assigned to the 45th, but the 36th was another key division fighting in that area. The divisions were split up quickly upon arrival to the front lines.

I was assigned to the 45th Infantry Division at Anzio in April 1944. Specifically, I joined C Company of the 157th Infantry Regiment within that division. The 45th was heavily engaged in some of the toughest fighting during the Italian campaign, and I spent several months with them.

Within the 45th Infantry Division, I was assigned to the 157th Infantry Regiment, C Company. I joined them at the front lines shortly after arriving at Anzio. The regiment was involved in continuous fighting and was my primary unit during the Italian campaign.

After some rest following the Rome campaign, we went down to Salerno, south of Naples, for amphibious training. This was in preparation for the invasion of southern France. Salerno was an important training and staging area for that operation in mid-1944.

I participated in the invasion of southern France in August 1944, landing on the second wave. We faced some German resistance but managed to push inland quickly. It was a major operation following our time in Italy and part of the broader Allied advance in Europe.

During the Italian campaign, Pope Pius XII declared Rome an open city, which meant no fighting should occur there. This declaration led to the German withdrawal and allowed us to take the city with less resistance. It was a significant moment that brought some relief after intense fighting.
